name: Maternal GFR in first trimester
tier_rationale: Tier A from Davison & Hytten 1974 BJOG: foundational study of GFR across pregnancy. The ~50% rise above non-pregnant baseline (~90 mL/min) is well-replicated across multiple subsequent studies (cited in Cheung & Lafayette 2013 review). Paywall-limited; specific first-trimester table re-verification pending.

source_check.source_citation: Cheung & Lafayette 2013, Renal Physiology of Pregnancy, Adv Chronic Kidney Dis (PMC4089195)
source_check.quote: creatinine clearance increased 20% at 4 weeks post-menses, 25% as early as gestational week 4, and 45% by gestational week 9
source_check.extracted_value: Review reports creatinine clearance (a GFR proxy) rising ~25% by gestational week 4 and ~45% by week 9; applied to a non-pregnant baseline of ~90-100 mL/min this yields a first-trimester GFR of roughly 120-135 mL/min, bracketing the stored central of 135 at its upper edge.

notes: First-trimester GFR, ~50% above non-pregnant baseline of ~90. The Davison 1974 paper is the foundational citation; multiple downstream reviews (including Cheung 2013) confirm the magnitude of rise.

name: Maternal glomerular filtration rate at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 ACKD review: GFR rises ~50% in pregnancy (~135 mL/min at peak). Multi-source review synthesis; foundational primary studies (e.g., Davison & Hytten 1974) are the cited evidence base.
notes: GFR at term; elevated approximately 40-50% above non-pregnant baseline.

name: Maternal plasma creatinine at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 ACKD review: GFR increases ~50% in pregnancy, producing a corresponding fall in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid. The review's Table 1 reports a representative average serum creatinine of 0.5 mg/dL during pregnancy. Tier A reflects the review's synthesis of multiple sources; term-specific values may differ from the pregnancy-averaged figure.

source_check.source_citation: Wiles et al. 2019, Serum Creatinine in Pregnancy: A Systematic Review, Kidney Int Rep (PMC6409397)
source_check.quote: serum creatinine values greater than 76 μmol/l (0.86 mg/dl) in the first trimester, 72 μmol/l (0.81 mg/dl) in the second trimester, and 77 μmol/l (0.87 mg/dl) in the third trimester should be considered to be outside the upper limit of normal
source_check.extracted_value: Systematic review of normal-pregnancy serum creatinine gives the third-trimester upper limit of normal as 77 umol/l = 0.87 mg/dL; the stored central 0.6 mg/dL (0.5-0.7) lies comfortably within the normal third-trimester range implied by this upper limit.

notes: Term serum creatinine. Cheung 2013 Table 1 lists 0.5 mg/dL as the average during pregnancy; the dataset's 0.6 at term is plausible given mid-third-trimester recovery toward non-pregnant baseline, but the precise term-vs-average distinction needs human re-verification.

name: Maternal non-pregnant GFR
tier_rationale: Tier A: non-pregnant GFR in healthy adult women is ~100 mL/min; this is the textbook baseline against which the ~50% pregnancy rise is measured (Conrad 2001, Davison 1974, Cheung 2013 review).

source_check.source_citation: Gounden, Bhatt & Jialal, Renal Function Tests, StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf NBK544228)
source_check.quote: The normal range for CrCl is age-dependent, but for younger, healthy adults, it is about 100 to 120 mL/min in males and 90 to 110 mL/min in females.
source_check.extracted_value: StatPearls gives normal creatinine clearance (a GFR proxy) for younger healthy adult females as 90 to 110 mL/min, matching the stored non-pregnant baseline central 100 and range 90-110.

name: Gestational week of peak GFR
tier_rationale: Tier B from Conrad 2001 mechanistic review and Dunlop 1981 longitudinal data: GFR peaks ~T1/early T2 driven by relaxin-mediated renal vasodilation.

name: Logistic GFR rise rate
tier_rationale: Tier C: empirical logistic-fit rate to Conrad 2001 / Dunlop 1981 trajectories. Single-fit value, no independent meta-analytic confirmation.

name: Maternal non-pregnant renal plasma flow
tier_rationale: Tier B from Dunlop 1981 longitudinal cohort: non-pregnant RPF ~600 mL/min.

name: Maternal peak renal plasma flow
tier_rationale: Tier B from Dunlop 1981: RPF peaks ~50-70% above baseline in T2, declines somewhat by term.

name: Week of peak renal plasma flow
tier_rationale: Tier B from Dunlop 1981: RPF peak ~24 weeks; the bell-shaped trajectory falls toward term.

name: Filtration fraction at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung 2013 review: FF drops in mid-pregnancy (RPF rises faster than GFR) then partly recovers; ~0.18 at term.

source_check.source_citation: Physiology, Renal Blood Flow and Filtration, StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf NBK482248)
source_check.quote: Filtration fraction (FF) is the fraction of renal plasma flow (RPF) filtered across the glomerulus. The equation is GFR divided by RPF. FF is about 20%
source_check.extracted_value: StatPearls defines filtration fraction as GFR/RPF and gives the normal (non-pregnant) value as about 20% (~0.20). Filtration fraction dips early in pregnancy (RPF rises faster than GFR) then returns toward this ~0.20 baseline by the third trimester, consistent with the stored term value of 0.18 just below 0.20.

name: Plasma osmolality drop from baseline
tier_rationale: Tier A from Davison 1981: plasma osmolality falls ~10 mOsm/kg in early pregnancy and remains low through term. Well-replicated; the osmotic-threshold-reset is the underlying mechanism.

source_check.quote: plasma osmolality (Posm) … was 10 mosmol/kg lower than preconception values by the tenth week, changing little thereafter.
source_check.extracted_value: Abstract explicitly reports plasma osmolality fell to 10 mOsm/kg below preconception by the tenth week (and 9 mOsm/kg lower in the last trimester vs postpartum), matching the stored 10 mOsm/kg drop.

name: Plasma sodium drop from baseline
tier_rationale: Tier A from Davison 1981: plasma sodium falls ~5 mEq/L in pregnancy, reflecting the osmolality reset.

name: Blood urea nitrogen at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Cheung 2013 review: BUN falls from ~13 mg/dL non-pregnant to ~8 mg/dL in pregnancy due to GFR rise + plasma volume expansion.

source_check.source_citation: Cheung & Lafayette 2013, Renal Physiology of Pregnancy, Adv Chronic Kidney Dis, Table 1 (PMC4089195)
source_check.extracted_value: Review Table 1 of typical laboratory values during pregnancy lists blood urea nitrogen as 9.0 mg/dL, which falls within the stored range 6-11 and near the stored central of 8.

name: Urinary protein excretion at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013: increased GFR and modest glomerular leakage raise 24-hour urinary protein excretion from a non-pregnant baseline of ~40-80 mg to ~150 mg by term. The clinical threshold for proteinuria in pregnancy is 300 mg/24h, chosen so that the physiological rise alone does not classify a normal pregnancy as proteinuric.

source_check.source_citation: Conti-Ramsden et al. 2018 review (PMC5933079) citing Higby et al. 1994 normal-pregnancy values
source_check.quote: Higby et al subsequently evaluated 270 healthy pregnant women and reported the 95th percentile for 24-hour urinary protein excretion to be 259.4 mg.
source_check.extracted_value: Review reports that Higby et al studied 270 healthy pregnant women and found the 95th percentile for 24-hour urinary protein excretion to be 259.4 mg; this upper bound matches the stored upper range value of 260 mg/24h, with the stored central 150 falling within the normal-pregnancy distribution.

notes: Clinical proteinuria threshold (300 mg/24h) sits above the upper end of the physiological range.

name: Plasma uric acid, mid-pregnancy nadir
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013: plasma uric acid falls in early-to-mid pregnancy from a non-pregnant baseline of ~4-5 mg/dL to a nadir of ~2.5-3.5 mg/dL by ~22-24 weeks, driven by increased GFR and renal uric-acid clearance. The fall is one of the more reproducible mid-pregnancy biochemical changes.

source_check.source_citation: Cheung & Lafayette 2013, Renal Physiology of Pregnancy, Adv Chronic Kidney Dis (PMC4089195)
source_check.quote: Serum uric acid levels fall in early pregnancy, reaching a nadir of 2 to 3.0 mg/dL by 22 to 24 weeks, followed by gradual rise to normal by term.
source_check.extracted_value: Review states serum uric acid falls in early pregnancy to a nadir of 2 to 3.0 mg/dL by 22 to 24 weeks; the stored central 3.0 mg/dL (2.5-3.5) sits at the top of this quoted nadir band.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, mid-pregnancy nadir (~22-24 weeks)

name: Plasma uric acid at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013: plasma uric acid rises back through the third trimester, reaching ~4-5 mg/dL by term as net renal clearance falls toward non-pregnant values. Term levels >6 mg/dL are clinically suggestive of preeclampsia and are used as a supporting biochemical marker, illustrating how the same number means different things in early vs late pregnancy.

source_check.source_citation: Gebrie et al. 2022, normotensive control group, third-trimester case-control study (PMC9593197)
source_check.extracted_value: The normotensive control group (mean gestational age 37.98 +/- 3.83 weeks, i.e. third trimester/term) had a mean serum uric acid of 4.43 +/- 1.38 mg/dL, consistent with the stored term central 4.5 mg/dL and within the stored range 3.5-5.5.

name: Cumulative sodium retention across pregnancy
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 (renal physiology in pregnancy review): the mother retains roughly 900–1000 mg of total sodium across normal pregnancy, distributed across the expanded plasma volume and the gravid uterus/fetus/amniotic fluid. The number is a derived sum across the trimesters and is consistent within ~20% across the renal-in-pregnancy review literature.

source_check.quote: A rise in serum aldosterone results in a net gain of approximately 1000 mg of sodium.
source_check.extracted_value: Abstract states a rise in aldosterone produces a net gain of approximately 1000 mg of sodium, equal to the stored 1.0 g cumulative sodium retention.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, cumulative across 40 weeks

notes: Despite the substantial sodium retention, plasma sodium concentration falls ~5 mEq/L because plasma-volume expansion outpaces solute retention.

name: 24-hour urinary glucose excretion at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Davison & Hytten 1974 J Obstet Gynaecol Br Commonw: glucosuria of pregnancy is intermittent but real — the combination of a higher filtered glucose load (rising GFR) and reduced fractional tubular reabsorption can produce 24-hour urinary glucose well above the non-pregnant ~50 mg/24h. The distribution is wide and right-skewed; values up to a few hundred mg/24h are within normal pregnancy and do not by themselves indicate glucose intolerance.
notes: Random-spot dipstick glucosuria in pregnancy is poorly specific for diabetes; gestational-diabetes screening uses a glucose tolerance test, not urine.

name: Tubular phosphate threshold (TmP/GFR) at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 review: the renal phosphate threshold (TmP/GFR) at term is broadly preserved at ~0.85 mmol/L, similar to non-pregnant values, despite the substantial rise in GFR. Quantitative human data are sparse outside this review; mechanism (PTH/PTHrP/FGF23 balance) is well-characterised qualitatively.
notes: Phosphate handling in pregnancy is mostly normal; clinically relevant changes are restricted to coexistent renal or parathyroid disease.
